title: [v5.4] Backport linter update
body:
Macos build task is failing in the lint step currently, I suspect because the macos workers were updated to go 1.24 but the older golangci-lint version did not support it yet. As such we have to update to the new version here.
clean cherry picks from #25304 and #25315

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *Luap99* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/v5.4/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

@Luap99 Should we put this in v5.4-rhel too?
/lgtm

> @Luap99 Should we put this in v5.4-rhel too?

Unless we plan to update our CI images to go 1.24 we do not need it, it is only needed because the macos workers were updated. But all windows and macos test should already be disabled in the rhel branch so this will not block things, ref https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/25306
And because this contains code changes I cannot guarantee that this is bug free, I am very sure the changes are harmless but still I assume for the RHEL process we would need exceptions? Easier to not worry about it.
Fair enough... It'd almost be worth it to prevent cherry-pick conflicts for future backports, but not enough to justify going through the RHEL process
